skill.name: game-design
skill.description: Game design principles. GDD structure, balancing, player psychology, progression.
skill.summary: Master game design fundamentals including core loops, GDD structure, player motivation, difficulty balancing, and progression systems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is documentation-only describing game design principles. Pattern matches for 'external_commands' were triggered by markdown code blocks (triple backticks) used for formatting diagrams and examples. Pattern matches for 'weak cryptographic algorithm' had no basis - no cryptographic code exists in the file. The skill only uses Read, Glob, and Grep tools with no code execution, network access, or filesystem modifications.

content.user_title: Design engaging games with proven design principles
content.value_statement: Game designers struggle with structure and player engagement. This skill provides frameworks for core loops, GDD creation, balancing, and progression that keep players in flow state.
actual_capabilities: Structure comprehensive game design documents with all essential sections
Design core gameplay loops that pass the 30-second fun test
Apply player psychology principles for different motivation types
Balance difficulty to maintain player flow state
Create progression systems that reward without frustrating
Identify and avoid common game design anti-patterns
limitations: Does not generate actual game code or assets
Focused on design theory, not engine-specific implementation
Best suited for conceptual and documentation phases

use_cases.title: Indie Game Developer
use_cases.description: Structure your first game design document and establish core mechanics before writing code

use_cases.title: Game Design Student
use_cases.description: Learn industry-standard GDD structure and player psychology frameworks

use_cases.title: Producer Reviewing Designs
use_cases.description: Evaluate design documents for completeness and identify potential engagement issues

prompt_templates.title: Create Core Loop
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a 30-second core gameplay loop for a [GENRE] game where the player [PLAYER GOAL]. Include action, feedback, reward, and repeat elements.

prompt_templates.title: Structure GDD
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a game design document outline for a game about [CONCEPT]. Include pitch, core loop, mechanics, progression, art style, and audio sections.

prompt_templates.title: Balance Difficulty
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this gameplay scenario for flow state: [SCENARIO]. Suggest adjustments to keep challenge matched to player skill level.

prompt_templates.title: Design Progression System
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a progression system for a [GAME TYPE] that combines skill-based, power-based, and content-based advancement. Define milestones at 1, 5, and 10 hour play marks.

output_examples.input: Design a core loop for a mobile puzzle game about gardening
output_examples.output: Core Loop: Select seed type → Plant in plot → Wait/watch growth animation → Harvest mature plants → Earn coins and unlock new seeds. Feedback includes visual growth stages and satisfying harvest effects. Rewards escalate from common to rare plant varieties.

output_examples.input: What progression works for a roguelike deckbuilder?
output_examples.output: Layered progression: (1) Skill - player learns enemy patterns and card synergies, (2) Power - unlock new cards and relics between runs, (3) Content - new characters and biomes unlock via achievements, (4) Story - narrative fragments revealed through boss defeats.

best_practices: Playtest early and often - fun is discovered through iteration, not designed on paper
Start with a small, polished core loop before expanding mechanics
Design for multiple player motivation types to broaden appeal
anti_patterns: Designing in isolation without player feedback
Polishing mechanics before confirming they are fun
Forcing a single playstyle instead of allowing player expression

faq.question: What is a game design document (GDD)?
faq.answer: A GDD is a living document that describes your game's vision, mechanics, and systems. It includes the pitch, core loop, gameplay mechanics, progression, art style, and audio direction. Keep it updated as the game evolves.

faq.question: How do I know if my core loop is fun?
faq.answer: Apply the 30-second test: can a player do something enjoyable in 30 seconds that they want to repeat? Playtest this loop in isolation before building surrounding systems.

faq.question: What is flow state in game design?
faq.answer: Flow state occurs when challenge matches player skill - not too hard (frustration) and not too easy (boredom). Dynamic difficulty, difficulty selection, and accessibility options help maintain flow.

faq.question: How should I structure player progression?
faq.answer: Use layered progression: skill-based (player improves), power-based (character strengthens), content-based (new areas unlock), and story-based (narrative advances). Combine multiple types for engagement.

faq.question: What player types should I design for?
faq.answer: Consider four motivation types: Achievers (goals and completion), Explorers (discovery and secrets), Socializers (interaction and community), and Competitors (competition and dominance). Most games appeal to 2-3 types.

faq.question: When should I start playtesting?
faq.answer: As soon as you have a playable prototype of your core loop. Early playtesting reveals fundamental fun issues before you invest in polish. Iterate based on feedback constantly.
